694: /// The receiving side of a channel.
695: ///
696: /// # Examples
697: ///
698: /// ```
699: /// use std::thread;
700: /// use std::time::Duration;
701: /// use crossbeam_channel::unbounded;
702: ///
703: /// let (s, r) = unbounded();
704: ///
705: /// thread::spawn(move || {
706: ///     let _ = s.send(1);
707: ///     thread::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1));
708: ///     let _ = s.send(2);
709: /// });
710: ///
711: /// assert_eq!(r.recv(), Ok(1)); // Received immediately.
712: /// assert_eq!(r.recv(), Ok(2)); // Received after 1 second.
713: /// ```
714: pub struct Receiver<T> {
715:     flavor: ReceiverFlavor<T>,
716: }
717: 
718: /// Receiver flavors.
719: enum ReceiverFlavor<T> {
720:     /// Bounded channel based on a preallocated array.
721:     Array(counter::Receiver<flavors::array::Channel<T>>),
722: 
723:     /// Unbounded channel implemented as a linked list.
724:     List(counter::Receiver<flavors::list::Channel<T>>),
725: 
726:     /// Zero-capacity channel.
727:     Zero(counter::Receiver<flavors::zero::Channel<T>>),
728: 
729:     /// The after flavor.
730:     At(Arc<flavors::at::Channel>),
731: 
732:     /// The tick flavor.
733:     Tick(Arc<flavors::tick::Channel>),
734: 
735:     /// The never flavor.
736:     Never(flavors::never::Channel<T>),
737: }
738: 
739: unsafe impl<T: Send> Send for Receiver<T> {}
740: unsafe impl<T: Send> Sync for Receiver<T> {}
741: 
742: impl<T> UnwindSafe for Receiver<T> {}
743: impl<T> RefUnwindSafe for Receiver<T> {}
744: 
745: impl<T> Receiver<T> {
746:     /// Attempts to receive a message from the channel without blocking.
747:     ///
748:     /// This method will either receive a message from the channel immediately or return an error
749:     /// if the channel is empty.
750:     ///
751:     /// If called on a zero-capacity channel, this method will receive a message only if there
752:     /// happens to be a send operation on the other side of the channel at the same time.
753:     ///
754:     /// # Examples
755:     ///
756:     /// ```
757:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{unbounded, TryRecvError};
758:     ///
759:     /// let (s, r) = unbounded();
760:     /// assert_eq!(r.try_recv(), Err(TryRecvError::Empty));
761:     ///
762:     /// s.send(5).unwrap();
763:     /// drop(s);
764:     ///
765:     /// assert_eq!(r.try_recv(), Ok(5));
766:     /// assert_eq!(r.try_recv(), Err(TryRecvError::Disconnected));
767:     /// ```
768:     pub fn try_recv(&self) -> Result<T, TryRecvError> {
769:         match &self.flavor {
770:             ReceiverFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.try_recv(),
771:             ReceiverFlavor::List(chan) => chan.try_recv(),
772:             ReceiverFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.try_recv(),
773:             ReceiverFlavor::At(chan) => {
774:                 let msg = chan.try_recv();
775:                 unsafe {
776:                     mem::transmute_copy::<Result<Instant, TryRecvError>, Result<T, TryRecvError>>(
777:                         &msg,
778:                     )
779:                 }
780:             }
781:             ReceiverFlavor::Tick(chan) => {
782:                 let msg = chan.try_recv();
783:                 unsafe {
784:                     mem::transmute_copy::<Result<Instant, TryRecvError>, Result<T, TryRecvError>>(
785:                         &msg,
786:                     )
787:                 }
788:             }
789:             ReceiverFlavor::Never(chan) => chan.try_recv(),
790:         }
791:     }
792: 
793:     /// Blocks the current thread until a message is received or the channel is empty and
794:     /// disconnected.
795:     ///
796:     /// If the channel is empty and not disconnected, this call will block until the receive
797:     /// operation can proceed. If the channel is empty and becomes disconnected, this call will
798:     /// wake up and return an error.
799:     ///
800:     /// If called on a zero-capacity channel, this method will wait for a send operation to appear
801:     /// on the other side of the channel.
802:     ///
803:     /// # Examples
804:     ///
805:     /// ```
806:     /// use std::thread;
807:     /// use std::time::Duration;
808:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{unbounded, RecvError};
809:     ///
810:     /// let (s, r) = unbounded();
811:     ///
812:     /// thread::spawn(move || {
813:     ///     thread::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1));
814:     ///     s.send(5).unwrap();
815:     ///     drop(s);
816:     /// });
817:     ///
818:     /// assert_eq!(r.recv(), Ok(5));
819:     /// assert_eq!(r.recv(), Err(RecvError));
820:     /// ```
821:     pub fn recv(&self) -> Result<T, RecvError> {
822:         match &self.flavor {
823:             ReceiverFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.recv(None),
824:             ReceiverFlavor::List(chan) => chan.recv(None),
825:             ReceiverFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.recv(None),
826:             ReceiverFlavor::At(chan) => {
827:                 let msg = chan.recv(None);
828:                 unsafe {
829:                     mem::transmute_copy::<
830:                         Result<Instant, RecvTimeoutError>,
831:                         Result<T, RecvTimeoutError>,
832:                     >(&msg)
833:                 }
834:             }
835:             ReceiverFlavor::Tick(chan) => {
836:                 let msg = chan.recv(None);
837:                 unsafe {
838:                     mem::transmute_copy::<
839:                         Result<Instant, RecvTimeoutError>,
840:                         Result<T, RecvTimeoutError>,
841:                     >(&msg)
842:                 }
843:             }
844:             ReceiverFlavor::Never(chan) => chan.recv(None),
845:         }
846:         .map_err(|_| RecvError)
847:     }
848: 
849:     /// Waits for a message to be received from the channel, but only for a limited time.
850:     ///
851:     /// If the channel is empty and not disconnected, this call will block until the receive
852:     /// operation can proceed or the operation times out. If the channel is empty and becomes
853:     /// disconnected, this call will wake up and return an error.
854:     ///
855:     /// If called on a zero-capacity channel, this method will wait for a send operation to appear
856:     /// on the other side of the channel.
857:     ///
858:     /// # Examples
859:     ///
860:     /// ```
861:     /// use std::thread;
862:     /// use std::time::Duration;
863:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{unbounded, RecvTimeoutError};
864:     ///
865:     /// let (s, r) = unbounded();
866:     ///
867:     /// thread::spawn(move || {
868:     ///     thread::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1));
869:     ///     s.send(5).unwrap();
870:     ///     drop(s);
871:     /// });
872:     ///
873:     /// assert_eq!(
874:     ///     r.recv_timeout(Duration::from_millis(500)),
875:     ///     Err(RecvTimeoutError::Timeout),
876:     /// );
877:     /// assert_eq!(
878:     ///     r.recv_timeout(Duration::from_secs(1)),
879:     ///     Ok(5),
880:     /// );
881:     /// assert_eq!(
882:     ///     r.recv_timeout(Duration::from_secs(1)),
883:     ///     Err(RecvTimeoutError::Disconnected),
884:     /// );
885:     /// ```
886:     pub fn recv_timeout(&self, timeout: Duration) -> Result<T, RecvTimeoutError> {
887:         match Instant::now().checked_add(timeout) {
888:             Some(deadline) => self.recv_deadline(deadline),
889:             None => self.recv().map_err(RecvTimeoutError::from),
890:         }
891:     }
892: 
893:     /// Waits for a message to be received from the channel, but only before a given deadline.
894:     ///
895:     /// If the channel is empty and not disconnected, this call will block until the receive
896:     /// operation can proceed or the operation times out. If the channel is empty and becomes
897:     /// disconnected, this call will wake up and return an error.
898:     ///
899:     /// If called on a zero-capacity channel, this method will wait for a send operation to appear
900:     /// on the other side of the channel.
901:     ///
902:     /// # Examples
903:     ///
904:     /// ```
905:     /// use std::thread;
906:     /// use std::time::{Instant, Duration};
907:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{unbounded, RecvTimeoutError};
908:     ///
909:     /// let (s, r) = unbounded();
910:     ///
911:     /// thread::spawn(move || {
912:     ///     thread::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1));
913:     ///     s.send(5).unwrap();
914:     ///     drop(s);
915:     /// });
916:     ///
917:     /// let now = Instant::now();
918:     ///
919:     /// assert_eq!(
920:     ///     r.recv_deadline(now + Duration::from_millis(500)),
921:     ///     Err(RecvTimeoutError::Timeout),
922:     /// );
923:     /// assert_eq!(
924:     ///     r.recv_deadline(now + Duration::from_millis(1500)),
925:     ///     Ok(5),
926:     /// );
927:     /// assert_eq!(
928:     ///     r.recv_deadline(now + Duration::from_secs(5)),
929:     ///     Err(RecvTimeoutError::Disconnected),
930:     /// );
931:     /// ```
932:     pub fn recv_deadline(&self, deadline: Instant) -> Result<T, RecvTimeoutError> {
933:         match &self.flavor {
934:             ReceiverFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.recv(Some(deadline)),
935:             ReceiverFlavor::List(chan) => chan.recv(Some(deadline)),
936:             ReceiverFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.recv(Some(deadline)),
937:             ReceiverFlavor::At(chan) => {
938:                 let msg = chan.recv(Some(deadline));
939:                 unsafe {
940:                     mem::transmute_copy::<
941:                         Result<Instant, RecvTimeoutError>,
942:                         Result<T, RecvTimeoutError>,
943:                     >(&msg)
944:                 }
945:             }
946:             ReceiverFlavor::Tick(chan) => {
947:                 let msg = chan.recv(Some(deadline));
948:                 unsafe {
949:                     mem::transmute_copy::<
950:                         Result<Instant, RecvTimeoutError>,
951:                         Result<T, RecvTimeoutError>,
952:                     >(&msg)
953:                 }
954:             }
955:             ReceiverFlavor::Never(chan) => chan.recv(Some(deadline)),
956:         }
957:     }
958: 
959:     /// Returns `true` if the channel is empty.
960:     ///
961:     /// Note: Zero-capacity channels are always empty.
962:     ///
963:     /// # Examples
964:     ///
965:     /// ```
966:     /// use crossbeam_channel::unbounded;
967:     ///
968:     /// let (s, r) = unbounded();
969:     ///
970:     /// assert!(r.is_empty());
971:     /// s.send(0).unwrap();
972:     /// assert!(!r.is_empty());
973:     /// ```
974:     pub fn is_empty(&self) -> bool {
975:         match &self.flavor {
976:             ReceiverFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.is_empty(),
977:             ReceiverFlavor::List(chan) => chan.is_empty(),
978:             ReceiverFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.is_empty(),
979:             ReceiverFlavor::At(chan) => chan.is_empty(),
980:             ReceiverFlavor::Tick(chan) => chan.is_empty(),
981:             ReceiverFlavor::Never(chan) => chan.is_empty(),
982:         }
983:     }

985:     /// Returns `true` if the channel is full.
986:     ///
987:     /// Note: Zero-capacity channels are always full.
988:     ///
989:     /// # Examples
990:     ///
991:     /// ```
992:     /// use crossbeam_channel::bounded;
993:     ///
994:     /// let (s, r) = bounded(1);
995:     ///
996:     /// assert!(!r.is_full());
997:     /// s.send(0).unwrap();
998:     /// assert!(r.is_full());
999:     /// ```
1000:     pub fn is_full(&self) -> bool {
1001:         match &self.flavor {
1002:             ReceiverFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.is_full(),
1003:             ReceiverFlavor::List(chan) => chan.is_full(),
1004:             ReceiverFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.is_full(),
1005:             ReceiverFlavor::At(chan) => chan.is_full(),
1006:             ReceiverFlavor::Tick(chan) => chan.is_full(),
1007:             ReceiverFlavor::Never(chan) => chan.is_full(),
1008:         }
1009:     }
1010: 
1011:     /// Returns the number of messages in the channel.
1012:     ///
1013:     /// # Examples
1014:     ///
1015:     /// ```
1016:     /// use crossbeam_channel::unbounded;
1017:     ///
1018:     /// let (s, r) = unbounded();
1019:     /// assert_eq!(r.len(), 0);
1020:     ///
1021:     /// s.send(1).unwrap();
1022:     /// s.send(2).unwrap();
1023:     /// assert_eq!(r.len(), 2);
1024:     /// ```
1025:     pub fn len(&self) -> usize {
1026:         match &self.flavor {
1027:             ReceiverFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.len(),
1028:             ReceiverFlavor::List(chan) => chan.len(),
1029:             ReceiverFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.len(),
1030:             ReceiverFlavor::At(chan) => chan.len(),
1031:             ReceiverFlavor::Tick(chan) => chan.len(),
1032:             ReceiverFlavor::Never(chan) => chan.len(),
1033:         }
1034:     }

1036:     /// If the channel is bounded, returns its capacity.
1037:     ///
1038:     /// # Examples
1039:     ///
1040:     /// ```
1041:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{bounded, unbounded};
1042:     ///
1043:     /// let (_, r) = unbounded::<i32>();
1044:     /// assert_eq!(r.capacity(), None);
1045:     ///
1046:     /// let (_, r) = bounded::<i32>(5);
1047:     /// assert_eq!(r.capacity(), Some(5));

1052:     pub fn capacity(&self) -> Option<usize> {

1054:             ReceiverFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.capacity(),
1055:             ReceiverFlavor::List(chan) => chan.capacity(),
1056:             ReceiverFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.capacity(),
1057:             ReceiverFlavor::At(chan) => chan.capacity(),

1063:     /// A blocking iterator over messages in the channel.
1064:     ///
1065:     /// Each call to [`next`] blocks waiting for the next message and then returns it. However, if
1066:     /// the channel becomes empty and disconnected, it returns [`None`] without blocking.
1067:     ///
1068:     /// [`next`]: Iterator::next
1069:     ///
1070:     /// # Examples
1071:     ///
1072:     /// ```
1073:     /// use std::thread;
1074:     /// use crossbeam_channel::unbounded;
1075:     ///
1076:     /// let (s, r) = unbounded();
1077:     ///
1078:     /// thread::spawn(move || {
1079:     ///     s.send(1).unwrap();
1080:     ///     s.send(2).unwrap();
1081:     ///     s.send(3).unwrap();
1082:     ///     drop(s); // Disconnect the channel.
1083:     /// });
1084:     ///
1085:     /// // Collect all messages from the channel.
1086:     /// // Note that the call to `collect` blocks until the sender is dropped.
1087:     /// let v: Vec<_> = r.iter().collect();
1088:     ///
1089:     /// assert_eq!(v, [1, 2, 3]);
1090:     /// ```
1091:     pub fn iter(&self) -> Iter<'_, T> {
1092:         Iter { receiver: self }
1093:     }
1094: 
1095:     /// A non-blocking iterator over messages in the channel.
1096:     ///
1097:     /// Each call to [`next`] returns a message if there is one ready to be received. The iterator
1098:     /// never blocks waiting for the next message.
1099:     ///
1100:     /// [`next`]: Iterator::next
1101:     ///
1102:     /// # Examples
1103:     ///
1104:     /// ```
1105:     /// use std::thread;
1106:     /// use std::time::Duration;
1107:     /// use crossbeam_channel::unbounded;
1108:     ///
1109:     /// let (s, r) = unbounded::<i32>();
1110:     ///
1111:     /// # let t =
1112:     /// thread::spawn(move || {
1113:     ///     s.send(1).unwrap();
1114:     ///     thread::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1));
1115:     ///     s.send(2).unwrap();
1116:     ///     thread::sleep(Duration::from_secs(2));
1117:     ///     s.send(3).unwrap();
1118:     /// });
1119:     ///
1120:     /// thread::sleep(Duration::from_secs(2));
1121:     ///
1122:     /// // Collect all messages from the channel without blocking.
1123:     /// // The third message hasn't been sent yet so we'll collect only the first two.
1124:     /// let v: Vec<_> = r.try_iter().collect();
1125:     ///
1126:     /// assert_eq!(v, [1, 2]);
1127:     /// # t.join().unwrap(); // join thread to avoid https://github.com/rust-lang/miri/issues/1371
1128:     /// ```
1129:     pub fn try_iter(&self) -> TryIter<'_, T> {
1130:         TryIter { receiver: self }
1131:     }
1132: 
1133:     /// Returns `true` if receivers belong to the same channel.
1134:     ///
1135:     /// # Examples
1136:     ///
1137:     /// ```rust
1138:     /// use crossbeam_channel::unbounded;
1139:     ///
1140:     /// let (_, r) = unbounded::<usize>();
1141:     ///
1142:     /// let r2 = r.clone();
1143:     /// assert!(r.same_channel(&r2));
1144:     ///
1145:     /// let (_, r3) = unbounded();
1146:     /// assert!(!r.same_channel(&r3));
1147:     /// ```
1148:     pub fn same_channel(&self, other: &Self) -> bool {
1149:         match (&self.flavor, &other.flavor) {
1150:             (ReceiverFlavor::Array(a), ReceiverFlavor::Array(b)) => a == b,
1151:             (ReceiverFlavor::List(a), ReceiverFlavor::List(b)) => a == b,
1152:             (ReceiverFlavor::Zero(a), ReceiverFlavor::Zero(b)) => a == b,
1153:             (ReceiverFlavor::At(a), ReceiverFlavor::At(b)) => Arc::ptr_eq(a, b),
1154:             (ReceiverFlavor::Tick(a), ReceiverFlavor::Tick(b)) => Arc::ptr_eq(a, b),
1155:             (ReceiverFlavor::Never(_), ReceiverFlavor::Never(_)) => true,
1156:             _ => false,
1157:         }
1158:     }
1159: }

1164:             match &self.flavor {
1165:                 ReceiverFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.release(|c| c.disconnect()),
1166:                 ReceiverFlavor::List(chan) => chan.release(|c| c.disconnect_receivers()),
1167:                 ReceiverFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.release(|c| c.disconnect()),
1168:                 ReceiverFlavor::At(_) => {}
1169:                 ReceiverFlavor::Tick(_) => {}
1170:                 ReceiverFlavor::Never(_) => {}
1171:             }
1172:         }
1173:     }
1174: }

1180:             ReceiverFlavor::List(chan) => ReceiverFlavor::List(chan.acquire()),
1181:             ReceiverFlavor::Zero(chan) => ReceiverFlavor::Zero(chan.acquire()),
1182:             ReceiverFlavor::At(chan) => ReceiverFlavor::At(chan.clone()),
1183:             ReceiverFlavor::Tick(chan) => ReceiverFlavor::Tick(chan.clone()),
1184:             ReceiverFlavor::Never(_) => ReceiverFlavor::Never(flavors::never::Channel::new()),
1185:         };
1186: 
1187:         Self { flavor }
1188:     }
1189: }

1215: /// A blocking iterator over messages in a channel.

1218: /// channel becomes empty and disconnected, it returns [`None`] without blocking.

<content>1: //! The `select!` macro.
2: 
3: /// A helper macro for `select!` to hide the long list of macro patterns from the documentation.
4: ///
5: /// The macro consists of two stages:
6: /// 1. Parsing
7: /// 2. Code generation
8: ///
9: /// The parsing stage consists of these subparts:
10: /// 1. `@list`: Turns a list of tokens into a list of cases.
11: /// 2. `@list_errorN`: Diagnoses the syntax error.
12: /// 3. `@case`: Parses a single case and verifies its argument list.
13: ///
14: /// The codegen stage consists of these subparts:
15: /// 1. `@init`: Attempts to optimize `select!` away and initializes the list of handles.
16: /// 1. `@count`: Counts the listed cases.
17: /// 3. `@add`: Adds send/receive operations to the list of handles and starts selection.
18: /// 4. `@complete`: Completes the selected send/receive operation.
19: ///
20: /// If the parsing stage encounters a syntax error or the codegen stage ends up with too many
21: /// cases to process, the macro fails with a compile-time error.
22: #[doc(hidden)]
23: #[macro_export]
24: macro_rules! crossbeam_channel_internal {
25:     // The list is empty. Now check the arguments of each processed case.
26:     (@list
27:         ()
28:         ($($head:tt)*)
29:     ) => {
30:         $crate::crossbeam_channel_internal!(
31:             @case
32:             ($($head)*)
33:             ()
34:             ()
35:         )
36:     };
37:     // If necessary, insert an empty argument list after `default`.
38:     (@list
39:         (default => $($tail:tt)*)
40:         ($($head:tt)*)
41:     ) => {
42:         $crate::crossbeam_channel_internal!(
43:             @list
44:             (default() => $($tail)*)
45:             ($($head)*)
46:         )
47:     };
48:     // But print an error if `default` is followed by a `->`.
49:     (@list
50:         (default -> $($tail:tt)*)
51:         ($($head:tt)*)
52:     ) => {
53:         compile_error!(
54:             "expected `=>` after `default` case, found `->`"
55:         )
56:     };
57:     // Print an error if there's an `->` after the argument list in the default case.
58:     (@list
59:         (default $args:tt -> $($tail:tt)*)
60:         ($($head:tt)*)
61:     ) => {
62:         compile_error!(
63:             "expected `=>` after `default` case, found `->`"
64:         )
65:     };
66:     // Print an error if there is a missing result in a recv case.
67:     (@list
68:         (recv($($args:tt)*) => $($tail:tt)*)
69:         ($($head:tt)*)
70:     ) => {
71:         compile_error!(
72:             "expected `->` after `recv` case, found `=>`"
73:         )
74:     };
75:     // Print an error if there is a missing result in a send case.
76:     (@list
77:         (send($($args:tt)*) => $($tail:tt)*)
78:         ($($head:tt)*)
79:     ) => {
80:         compile_error!(
81:             "expected `->` after `send` operation, found `=>`"
82:         )
83:     };
84:     // Make sure the arrow and the result are not repeated.
85:     (@list
86:         ($case:ident $args:tt -> $res:tt -> $($tail:tt)*)
87:         ($($head:tt)*)
88:     ) => {
89:         compile_error!("expected `=>`, found `->`")
90:     };
91:     // Print an error if there is a semicolon after the block.
92:     (@list
93:         ($case:ident $args:tt $(-> $res:pat)* => $body:block; $($tail:tt)*)
94:         ($($head:tt)*)
95:     ) => {
96:         compile_error!(
97:             "did you mean to put a comma instead of the semicolon after `}`?"
98:         )
99:     };
100:     // The first case is separated by a comma.
101:     (@list
102:         ($case:ident ($($args:tt)*) $(-> $res:pat)* => $body:expr, $($tail:tt)*)
103:         ($($head:tt)*)
104:     ) => {
105:         $crate::crossbeam_channel_internal!(
106:             @list
107:             ($($tail)*)
108:             ($($head)* $case ($($args)*) $(-> $res)* => { $body },)
109:         )
110:     };
111:     // Don't require a comma after the case if it has a proper block.
112:     (@list
113:         ($case:ident ($($args:tt)*) $(-> $res:pat)* => $body:block $($tail:tt)*)
114:         ($($head:tt)*)
115:     ) => {
116:         $crate::crossbeam_channel_internal!(
117:             @list
118:             ($($tail)*)
119:             ($($head)* $case ($($args)*) $(-> $res)* => { $body },)
120:         )
121:     };
122:     // Only one case remains.
123:     (@list
124:         ($case:ident ($($args:tt)*) $(-> $res:pat)* => $body:expr $(,)?)
125:         ($($head:tt)*)
126:     ) => {
127:         $crate::crossbeam_channel_internal!(
128:             @list
129:             ()
130:             ($($head)* $case ($($args)*) $(-> $res)* => { $body },)
131:         )
132:     };
133:     // Diagnose and print an error.
134:     (@list
135:         ($($tail:tt)*)
136:         ($($head:tt)*)
137:     ) => {
138:         $crate::crossbeam_channel_internal!(@list_error1 $($tail)*)
139:     };

155:                 "`",
156:             )
157:         )
158:     };

161:     };

171:     };
172:     (@list_error2 $case:ident => $($tail:tt)*) => {

180:     };

984: /// Selects from a set of channel operations.
985: ///
986: /// This macro allows you to define a set of channel operations, wait until any one of them becomes
987: /// ready, and finally execute it. If multiple operations are ready at the same time, a random one
988: /// among them is selected (i.e. the unbiased selection). Use [`select_biased!`] for the biased
989: /// selection.
990: ///
991: /// It is also possible to define a `default` case that gets executed if none of the operations are
992: /// ready, either right away or for a certain duration of time.
993: ///
994: /// An operation is considered to be ready if it doesn't have to block. Note that it is ready even
995: /// when it will simply return an error because the channel is disconnected.
996: ///
997: /// The `select!` macro is a convenience wrapper around [`Select`]. However, it cannot select over a
998: /// dynamically created list of channel operations.
999: ///
1000: /// [`Select`]: super::Select
1001: /// [`select_biased!`]: super::select_biased
1002: ///
1003: /// # Examples
1004: ///
1005: /// Block until a send or a receive operation is selected:
1006: ///
1007: /// ```
1008: /// use crossbeam_channel::{select, unbounded};
1009: ///
1010: /// let (s1, r1) = unbounded();
1011: /// let (s2, r2) = unbounded();
1012: /// s1.send(10).unwrap();
1013: ///
1014: /// // Since both operations are initially ready, a random one will be executed.
1015: /// select! {
1016: ///     recv(r1) -> msg => assert_eq!(msg, Ok(10)),
1017: ///     send(s2, 20) -> res => {
1018: ///         assert_eq!(res, Ok(()));
1019: ///         assert_eq!(r2.recv(), Ok(20));
1020: ///     }
1021: /// }
1022: /// ```
1023: ///
1024: /// Select from a set of operations without blocking:
1025: ///
1026: /// ```
1027: /// use std::thread;
1028: /// use std::time::Duration;
1029: /// use crossbeam_channel::{select, unbounded};
1030: ///
1031: /// let (s1, r1) = unbounded();
1032: /// let (s2, r2) = unbounded();
1033: ///
1034: /// # let t1 =
1035: /// thread::spawn(move || {
1036: ///     thread::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1));
1037: ///     s1.send(10).unwrap();
1038: /// });
1039: /// # let t2 =
1040: /// thread::spawn(move || {
1041: ///     thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(500));
1042: ///     s2.send(20).unwrap();
1043: /// });
1044: ///
1045: /// // None of the operations are initially ready.

part.text:
## Goal

Fix crossbeam-channel issue #1096: A `SelectedOperation` registered with one `Sender` or `Receiver` instance currently panics when completed using a clone of that handle, even though both handles belong to the same channel. Completion with a cloned handle from the same channel must work for unbounded, bounded, and zero-capacity channels, while a handle from a different channel must still be rejected. The fix must cover both the `Select` API and the `select!` macro machinery.

## Instructions

- Work offline, do not inspect git history or any remote
- Do not modify tests or documentation
- Implement a production-quality fix
- Run relevant tests before finishing

## Discoveries

The root cause is in how `SelectedOperation` validates the handle used to complete the operation. In `select.rs`:

1. **`SelectedOperation::send()`** (line 1267-1274): Checks `s as *const Sender<T> as *const u8 == self.ptr` ΓÇö a pointer equality check. This fails when using a cloned handle because the clone is at a different address.

2. **`SelectedOperation::recv()`** (line 1301-1309): Same issue ΓÇö `r as *const Receiver<T> as *const u8 == self.ptr`.

3. The `ptr` field is stored at registration time (set from the original handle's address in `Select::send()`/`Select::recv()` at lines 679 and 701).

4. In the `select!` macro (`select_macro.rs`), the `ptr` is also stored at the original address ΓÇö for recv at line 855: `$sel[$i] = ($var, $i, $var as *const $crate::Receiver<_> as *const u8)`, and for send at line 887: `$sel[$i] = ($var, $i, $var as *const $crate::Sender<_> as *const u8)`.

Key insight: `Sender` and `Receiver` each contain an internal flavor enum (`SenderFlavor` and `ReceiverFlavor`) which holds an `Arc`-wrapped, counter-based channel handle. Cloned `Sender`/`Receiver` handles share the same underlying channel object (the `Arc<counter::Sender<F>>` or `Arc<counter::Receiver<F>>`). The `same_channel()` methods on `Sender` and `Receiver` already compare the internal channel pointer appropriately.

The fix should replace the pointer equality check with a channel identity check: rather than comparing the handle address, we should compare whether the internal channel objects are the same. The `Sender::same_channel()` and `Receiver::same_channel()` methods provide this logic.

**Alternatives considered:**
- Store the channel identity (an opaque identifier derived from the internal `Arc`) instead of the raw pointer. This is the cleanest approach.
- For the `select!` macro, the generated code also stores the raw pointer in the tuple ΓÇö we need to change the third element of the handle tuple to carry channel identity instead of handle address.

The handle tuple `(&'a dyn SelectHandle, usize, *const u8)` uses the third element (`ptr`) solely for the identity check in `SelectedOperation::send()`/`recv()`. We can change this to carry an identifier that represents the channel rather than the handle address. However, since `SelectHandle` is a trait object and we don't want to couple it with channel identity, we need to pass channel identity separately.

**Recommended approach:** Store in `SelectedOperation` and in the handle tuple an opaque `*const ()` pointer that points to the underlying channel (the inner `counter::Sender<T>` or `counter::Receiver<T>`) instead of the `Sender`/`Receiver` handle wrapper. This way, all clones of the same channel will have the same inner pointer.

For `Sender`, the inner channel pointer for Array/List/Zero flavors is obtained via `match &self.flavor { SenderFlavor::Array(chan) => chan as *const _ as *const u8, ... }`. Same for `Receiver`.

## Accomplished

- Analyzed the `select.rs`, `select_macro.rs`, and `channel.rs` source files to understand the bug
- Identified the root cause: pointer equality check on `Sender`/`Receiver` handle addresses fails for cloned handles
- Identified the fix approach: replace the raw handle address in `SelectedOperation.ptr` and the handle tuple's `*const u8` with a pointer to the inner channel object (which is shared across clones)
- **No code changes have been made yet**

## Relevant files / directories

- `crossbeam-channel/src/select.rs` ΓÇö Contains `SelectedOperation` struct, `send()`/`recv()` completion methods, and the `Select` struct. **This is where the primary fix goes.**
- `crossbeam-channel/src/select_macro.rs` ΓÇö Contains the `select!` macro implementation that also stores handle pointer. **Needs corresponding changes.**
- `crossbeam-channel/src/channel.rs` ΓÇö Contains `Sender`, `Receiver`, their flavor enums, and `same_channel()` methods (reference for how channel identity works). Must ensure `Sender`/`Receiver` can expose inner channel pointer.
Continue if you have next steps, or stop and ask for clarification if you are unsure how to proceed.

part.text: Now let me read the `Sender`/`Receiver` struct definitions and their `same_channel` methods, plus the `SelectHandle` trait:

## Goal

Fix crossbeam-channel issue #1096: A `SelectedOperation` registered with one `Sender` or `Receiver` instance currently panics when completed using a *clone* of that handle, even though both handles belong to the same channel. Completion with a cloned handle from the same channel must work for unbounded, bounded, and zero-capacity channels, while a handle from a *different* channel must still be rejected. The fix must cover both the `Select` API and the `select!` macro machinery.

## Instructions

- Work offline, do not inspect git history or any remote source
- Do **not** modify tests or documentation
- Implement a production-quality fix
- Run relevant tests before finishing

## Discoveries

**Root cause:** In `select.rs`, `SelectedOperation::send()` and `SelectedOperation::recv()` validate the handle by comparing the address of the passed `Sender`/`Receiver` against `self.ptr` (the address stored at registration time). Since cloned `Sender`/`Receiver` handles are separate objects at different memory addresses, this pointer equality check fails.

**Fix approach:** Replace the raw handle address (`*const u8`) stored in `SelectedOperation.ptr` and in the `select!` macro handle tuples with a pointer to the **inner channel object** (the `counter::Sender<T>` or `counter::Receiver<T>` behind the flavor enum). Cloned `Sender`/`Receiver` handles share the same inner channel object, so comparing these inner pointers will correctly identify same-channel handles.

Implementation sketch:
1. In `channel.rs`, add a method to `Sender` and `Receiver` that returns the inner channel pointer (`*const u8`) from the appropriate flavor variant.
2. In `select.rs`, change `Select::send()` and `Select::recv()` to store the inner channel pointer instead of the handle address.
3. In `select.rs`, change `SelectedOperation::send()` and `SelectedOperation::recv()` to compare using the inner channel pointer instead of handle address.
4. In `select_macro.rs`, change the two `@add` arms (recv and send) to store the inner channel pointer instead of `$var as *const $crate::Receiver<_> as *const u8` / `$var as *const $crate::Sender<_> as *const u8`.
5. The `SelectHandle` trait and `Token` struct do **not** need modification ΓÇö only the `ptr` field's semantics change.

**Key files to read and understand before coding:**
- `channel.rs` lines 364ΓÇô714+: `Sender` struct, `SenderFlavor` enum, `Sender::same_channel()`
- `channel.rs` lines 714ΓÇô1150+: `Receiver` struct, `ReceiverFlavor` enum, `Receiver::same_channel()`
- `select.rs` lines 99+: `SelectHandle` trait
- `flavors/` directory to understand how each flavor stores its inner channel

## Accomplished

- Analyzed the source files (`select.rs`, `select_macro.rs`, `channel.rs`) to understand the bug
- Identified the root cause: pointer equality check on `Sender`/`Receiver` handle addresses fails for cloned handles
- Identified the fix approach: replace the raw handle address in `SelectedOperation.ptr` and the handle tuple's `*const u8` with a pointer to the inner channel object (which is shared across clones)
- **No code changes have been made yet**

## What needs to be done next

1. **Read the flavor implementations** to understand how each flavor stores its inner channel object ΓÇö specifically `flavors/array/mod.rs`, `flavors/list/mod.rs`, `flavors/zero/mod.rs`:
   - Need to see the `Channel` type for each flavor and how it's wrapped in `counter` types
   - Need to see the `SenderFlavor`/`ReceiverFlavor` variants and their inner types to know how to extract a pointer to the shared channel

2. **Read `channel.rs` more carefully** around the `same_channel()` implementations (lines 654 and 1148) ΓÇö they show how same-channel comparison already works across flavors; the fix should replicate that logic but return a `*const u8` instead of a `bool`.

3. **Implement the fix** in:
   - `crossbeam-channel/src/channel.rs` ΓÇö Add inner pointer methods to `Sender` and `Receiver`
   - `crossbeam-channel/src/select.rs` ΓÇö Change pointer storage and comparison
   - `crossbeam-channel/src/select_macro.rs` ΓÇö Change pointer storage in macro-generated code

4. **Build and test** to verify the fix works.

## Relevant files / directories

- `crossbeam-channel/src/select.rs` ΓÇö Primary fix location: `SelectedOperation` struct (line 1200+), `Select::send()`/`Select::recv()` (lines 677-705), `SelectedOperation::send()` (line 1267), `SelectedOperation::recv()` (line 1301)
- `crossbeam-channel/src/select_macro.rs` ΓÇö Macro counterpart: `@add` arms for recv (line 855) and send (line 887)
- `crossbeam-channel/src/channel.rs` ΓÇö `Sender`/`Receiver` structs and flavor enums; need to add a method to get inner channel pointer
- `crossbeam-channel/src/flavors/` ΓÇö Flavor implementations (array, list, zero, etc.); need to understand inner types for pointer extraction
Continue if you have next steps, or stop and ask for clarification if you are unsure how to proceed.

<content>364: pub struct Sender<T> {
365:     flavor: SenderFlavor<T>,
366: }
367: 
368: /// Sender flavors.
369: enum SenderFlavor<T> {
370:     /// Bounded channel based on a preallocated array.
371:     Array(counter::Sender<flavors::array::Channel<T>>),
372: 
373:     /// Unbounded channel implemented as a linked list.
374:     List(counter::Sender<flavors::list::Channel<T>>),
375: 
376:     /// Zero-capacity channel.
377:     Zero(counter::Sender<flavors::zero::Channel<T>>),
378: }
379: 
380: unsafe impl<T: Send> Send for Sender<T> {}
381: unsafe impl<T: Send> Sync for Sender<T> {}
382: 
383: impl<T> UnwindSafe for Sender<T> {}
384: impl<T> RefUnwindSafe for Sender<T> {}
385: 
386: impl<T> Sender<T> {
387:     /// Attempts to send a message into the channel without blocking.
388:     ///
389:     /// This method will either send a message into the channel immediately or return an error if
390:     /// the channel is full or disconnected. The returned error contains the original message.
391:     ///
392:     /// If called on a zero-capacity channel, this method will send the message only if there
393:     /// happens to be a receive operation on the other side of the channel at the same time.
394:     ///
395:     /// # Examples
396:     ///
397:     /// ```
398:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{bounded, TrySendError};
399:     ///
400:     /// let (s, r) = bounded(1);
401:     ///
402:     /// assert_eq!(s.try_send(1), Ok(()));
403:     /// assert_eq!(s.try_send(2), Err(TrySendError::Full(2)));
404:     ///
405:     /// drop(r);
406:     /// assert_eq!(s.try_send(3), Err(TrySendError::Disconnected(3)));
407:     /// ```
408:     pub fn try_send(&self, msg: T) -> Result<(), TrySendError<T>> {
409:         match &self.flavor {
410:             SenderFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.try_send(msg),
411:             SenderFlavor::List(chan) => chan.try_send(msg),
412:             SenderFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.try_send(msg),
413:         }
414:     }
415: 
416:     /// Blocks the current thread until a message is sent or the channel is disconnected.
417:     ///
418:     /// If the channel is full and not disconnected, this call will block until the send operation
419:     /// can proceed. If the channel becomes disconnected, this call will wake up and return an
420:     /// error. The returned error contains the original message.
421:     ///
422:     /// If called on a zero-capacity channel, this method will wait for a receive operation to
423:     /// appear on the other side of the channel.
424:     ///
425:     /// # Examples
426:     ///
427:     /// ```
428:     /// use std::thread;
429:     /// use std::time::Duration;
430:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{bounded, SendError};
431:     ///
432:     /// let (s, r) = bounded(1);
433:     /// assert_eq!(s.send(1), Ok(()));
434:     ///
435:     /// thread::spawn(move || {
436:     ///     assert_eq!(r.recv(), Ok(1));
437:     ///     thread::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1));
438:     ///     drop(r);
439:     /// });
440:     ///
441:     /// assert_eq!(s.send(2), Ok(()));
442:     /// assert_eq!(s.send(3), Err(SendError(3)));
443:     /// ```
444:     pub fn send(&self, msg: T) -> Result<(), SendError<T>> {
445:         match &self.flavor {
446:             SenderFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.send(msg, None),
447:             SenderFlavor::List(chan) => chan.send(msg, None),
448:             SenderFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.send(msg, None),
449:         }
450:         .map_err(|err| match err {
451:             SendTimeoutError::Disconnected(msg) => SendError(msg),
452:             SendTimeoutError::Timeout(_) => unreachable!(),
453:         })
454:     }
455: 
456:     /// Waits for a message to be sent into the channel, but only for a limited time.
457:     ///
458:     /// If the channel is full and not disconnected, this call will block until the send operation
459:     /// can proceed or the operation times out. If the channel becomes disconnected, this call will
460:     /// wake up and return an error. The returned error contains the original message.
461:     ///
462:     /// If called on a zero-capacity channel, this method will wait for a receive operation to
463:     /// appear on the other side of the channel.
464:     ///
465:     /// # Examples
466:     ///
467:     /// ```
468:     /// use std::thread;
469:     /// use std::time::Duration;
470:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{bounded, SendTimeoutError};
471:     ///
472:     /// let (s, r) = bounded(0);
473:     ///
474:     /// thread::spawn(move || {
475:     ///     thread::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1));
476:     ///     assert_eq!(r.recv(), Ok(2));
477:     ///     drop(r);
478:     /// });
479:     ///
480:     /// assert_eq!(
481:     ///     s.send_timeout(1, Duration::from_millis(500)),
482:     ///     Err(SendTimeoutError::Timeout(1)),
483:     /// );
484:     /// assert_eq!(
485:     ///     s.send_timeout(2, Duration::from_secs(1)),
486:     ///     Ok(()),
487:     /// );
488:     /// assert_eq!(
489:     ///     s.send_timeout(3, Duration::from_millis(500)),
490:     ///     Err(SendTimeoutError::Disconnected(3)),
491:     /// );
492:     /// ```
493:     pub fn send_timeout(&self, msg: T, timeout: Duration) -> Result<(), SendTimeoutError<T>> {
494:         match Instant::now().checked_add(timeout) {
495:             Some(deadline) => self.send_deadline(msg, deadline),
496:             None => self.send(msg).map_err(SendTimeoutError::from),
497:         }
498:     }
499: 
500:     /// Waits for a message to be sent into the channel, but only until a given deadline.
501:     ///
502:     /// If the channel is full and not disconnected, this call will block until the send operation
503:     /// can proceed or the operation times out. If the channel becomes disconnected, this call will
504:     /// wake up and return an error. The returned error contains the original message.
505:     ///
506:     /// If called on a zero-capacity channel, this method will wait for a receive operation to
507:     /// appear on the other side of the channel.
508:     ///
509:     /// # Examples
510:     ///
511:     /// ```
512:     /// use std::thread;
513:     /// use std::time::{Duration, Instant};
514:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{bounded, SendTimeoutError};
515:     ///
516:     /// let (s, r) = bounded(0);
517:     ///
518:     /// thread::spawn(move || {
519:     ///     thread::sleep(Duration::from_secs(1));
520:     ///     assert_eq!(r.recv(), Ok(2));
521:     ///     drop(r);
522:     /// });
523:     ///
524:     /// let now = Instant::now();
525:     ///
526:     /// assert_eq!(
527:     ///     s.send_deadline(1, now + Duration::from_millis(500)),
528:     ///     Err(SendTimeoutError::Timeout(1)),
529:     /// );
530:     /// assert_eq!(
531:     ///     s.send_deadline(2, now + Duration::from_millis(1500)),
532:     ///     Ok(()),
533:     /// );
534:     /// assert_eq!(
535:     ///     s.send_deadline(3, now + Duration::from_millis(2000)),
536:     ///     Err(SendTimeoutError::Disconnected(3)),
537:     /// );
538:     /// ```
539:     pub fn send_deadline(&self, msg: T, deadline: Instant) -> Result<(), SendTimeoutError<T>> {
540:         match &self.flavor {
541:             SenderFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.send(msg, Some(deadline)),
542:             SenderFlavor::List(chan) => chan.send(msg, Some(deadline)),
543:             SenderFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.send(msg, Some(deadline)),
544:         }
545:     }
546: 
547:     /// Returns `true` if the channel is empty.
548:     ///
549:     /// Note: Zero-capacity channels are always empty.
550:     ///
551:     /// # Examples
552:     ///
553:     /// ```
554:     /// use crossbeam_channel::unbounded;
555:     ///
556:     /// let (s, r) = unbounded();
557:     /// assert!(s.is_empty());
558:     ///
559:     /// s.send(0).unwrap();
560:     /// assert!(!s.is_empty());
561:     /// ```
562:     pub fn is_empty(&self) -> bool {
563:         match &self.flavor {
564:             SenderFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.is_empty(),
565:             SenderFlavor::List(chan) => chan.is_empty(),
566:             SenderFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.is_empty(),
567:         }
568:     }
569: 
570:     /// Returns `true` if the channel is full.
571:     ///
572:     /// Note: Zero-capacity channels are always full.
573:     ///
574:     /// # Examples
575:     ///
576:     /// ```
577:     /// use crossbeam_channel::bounded;
578:     ///
579:     /// let (s, r) = bounded(1);
580:     ///
581:     /// assert!(!s.is_full());
582:     /// s.send(0).unwrap();
583:     /// assert!(s.is_full());
584:     /// ```
585:     pub fn is_full(&self) -> bool {
586:         match &self.flavor {
587:             SenderFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.is_full(),
588:             SenderFlavor::List(chan) => chan.is_full(),
589:             SenderFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.is_full(),
590:         }
591:     }
592: 
593:     /// Returns the number of messages in the channel.
594:     ///
595:     /// # Examples
596:     ///
597:     /// ```
598:     /// use crossbeam_channel::unbounded;
599:     ///
600:     /// let (s, r) = unbounded();
601:     /// assert_eq!(s.len(), 0);
602:     ///
603:     /// s.send(1).unwrap();
604:     /// s.send(2).unwrap();
605:     /// assert_eq!(s.len(), 2);
606:     /// ```
607:     pub fn len(&self) -> usize {
608:         match &self.flavor {
609:             SenderFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.len(),
610:             SenderFlavor::List(chan) => chan.len(),
611:             SenderFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.len(),
612:         }
613:     }
614: 
615:     /// If the channel is bounded, returns its capacity.
616:     ///
617:     /// # Examples
618:     ///
619:     /// ```
620:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{bounded, unbounded};
621:     ///
622:     /// let (s, _) = unbounded::<i32>();
623:     /// assert_eq!(s.capacity(), None);
624:     ///
625:     /// let (s, _) = bounded::<i32>(5);
626:     /// assert_eq!(s.capacity(), Some(5));
627:     ///
628:     /// let (s, _) = bounded::<i32>(0);
629:     /// assert_eq!(s.capacity(), Some(0));
630:     /// ```
631:     pub fn capacity(&self) -> Option<usize> {
632:         match &self.flavor {
633:             SenderFlavor::Array(chan) => chan.capacity(),
634:             SenderFlavor::List(chan) => chan.capacity(),
635:             SenderFlavor::Zero(chan) => chan.capacity(),
636:         }
637:     }
638: 
639:     /// Returns `true` if senders belong to the same channel.
640:     ///
641:     /// # Examples
642:     ///
643:     /// ```rust
644:     /// use crossbeam_channel::unbounded;
645:     ///
646:     /// let (s, _) = unbounded::<usize>();
647:     ///
648:     /// let s2 = s.clone();
649:     /// assert!(s.same_channel(&s2));
650:     ///
651:     /// let (s3, _) = unbounded();
652:     /// assert!(!s.same_channel(&s3));
653:     /// ```
654:     pub fn same_channel(&self, other: &Self) -> bool {
655:         match (&self.flavor, &other.flavor) {
656:             (SenderFlavor::Array(ref a), SenderFlavor::Array(ref b)) => a == b,
657:             (SenderFlavor::List(ref a), SenderFlavor::List(ref b)) => a == b,
658:             (SenderFlavor::Zero(ref a), SenderFlavor::Zero(ref b)) => a == b,
659:             _ => false,
660:         }
661:     }
662: }

18: /// Temporary data that gets initialized during select or a blocking operation, and is consumed by
19: /// `read` or `write`.
20: ///
21: /// Each field contains data associated with a specific channel flavor.
22: // This is a private API that is used by the select macro.
23: #[derive(Debug, Default)]
24: pub struct Token {
25:     pub(crate) at: flavors::at::AtToken,
26:     pub(crate) array: flavors::array::ArrayToken,
27:     pub(crate) list: flavors::list::ListToken,
28:     #[allow(dead_code)]
29:     pub(crate) never: flavors::never::NeverToken,
30:     pub(crate) tick: flavors::tick::TickToken,
31:     pub(crate) zero: flavors::zero::ZeroToken,
32: }
33: 
34: /// Identifier associated with an operation by a specific thread on a specific channel.
35: #[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
36: pub struct Operation(usize);
37: 
38: impl Operation {
39:     /// Creates an operation identifier from a mutable reference.
40:     ///
41:     /// This function essentially just turns the address of the reference into a number. The
42:     /// reference should point to a variable that is specific to the thread and the operation,
43:     /// and is alive for the entire duration of select or blocking operation.
44:     #[inline]
45:     pub fn hook<T>(r: &mut T) -> Self {
46:         let val = r as *mut T as usize;
47:         // Make sure that the pointer address doesn't equal the numerical representation of
48:         // `Selected::{Waiting, Aborted, Disconnected}`.
49:         assert!(val > 2);
50:         Self(val)
51:     }
52: }
53: 
54: /// Current state of a select or a blocking operation.
55: #[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
56: pub enum Selected {
57:     /// Still waiting for an operation.
58:     Waiting,
59: 
60:     /// The attempt to block the current thread has been aborted.
61:     Aborted,
62: 
63:     /// An operation became ready because a channel is disconnected.
64:     Disconnected,
65: 
66:     /// An operation became ready because a message can be sent or received.
67:     Operation(Operation),
68: }
69: 
70: impl From<usize> for Selected {
71:     #[inline]
72:     fn from(val: usize) -> Self {
73:         match val {
74:             0 => Self::Waiting,
75:             1 => Self::Aborted,
76:             2 => Self::Disconnected,
77:             oper => Self::Operation(Operation(oper)),
78:         }
79:     }
80: }
81: 
82: impl From<Selected> for usize {
83:     #[inline]
84:     fn from(val: Selected) -> Self {
85:         match val {
86:             Selected::Waiting => 0,
87:             Selected::Aborted => 1,
88:             Selected::Disconnected => 2,
89:             Selected::Operation(Operation(val)) => val,
90:         }
91:     }
92: }
93:

95: ///
96: /// This is a handle that assists select in executing an operation, registration, deciding on the
97: /// appropriate deadline for blocking, etc.
98: // This is a private API (exposed inside crossbeam_channel::internal module) that is used by the select macro.
99: pub trait SelectHandle {

663:     /// Adds a send operation.
664:     ///
665:     /// Returns the index of the added operation.
666:     ///
667:     /// # Examples

670:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{unbounded, Select};

679:         let ptr = s as *const Sender<_> as *const u8;
680:         self.handles.push((s, i, ptr));
681:         self.next_index += 1;

685:     /// Adds a receive operation.
686:     ///
687:     /// Returns the index of the added operation.
688:     ///
689:     /// # Examples

692:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{unbounded, Select};
693:     ///
694:     /// let (s, r) = unbounded::<i32>();
695:     ///
696:     /// let mut sel = Select::new();
697:     /// let index = sel.recv(&r);
698:     /// ```
699:     pub fn recv<T>(&mut self, r: &'a Receiver<T>) -> usize {
700:         let i = self.next_index;
701:         let ptr = r as *const Receiver<_> as *const u8;
702:         self.handles.push((r, i, ptr));
703:         self.next_index += 1;
704:         i
705:     }

707:     /// Removes a previously added operation.
708:     ///
709:     /// This is useful when an operation is selected because the channel got disconnected and we
710:     /// want to try again to select a different operation instead.
711:     ///
712:     /// If new operations are added after removing some, the indices of removed operations will not
713:     /// be reused.
714:     ///
715:     /// # Panics
716:     ///
717:     /// An attempt to remove a non-existing or already removed operation will panic.
718:     ///
719:     /// # Examples
720:     ///
721:     /// ```
722:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{unbounded, Select};
723:     ///
724:     /// let (s1, r1) = unbounded::<i32>();
725:     /// let (_, r2) = unbounded::<i32>();
726:     ///
727:     /// let mut sel = Select::new();
728:     /// let oper1 = sel.recv(&r1);
729:     /// let oper2 = sel.recv(&r2);
730:     ///
731:     /// // Both operations are initially ready, so a random one will be executed.
732:     /// let oper = sel.select();
733:     /// assert_eq!(oper.index(), oper2);
734:     /// assert!(oper.recv(&r2).is_err());
735:     /// sel.remove(oper2);
736:     ///
737:     /// s1.send(10).unwrap();
738:     ///
739:     /// let oper = sel.select();
740:     /// assert_eq!(oper.index(), oper1);
741:     /// assert_eq!(oper.recv(&r1), Ok(10));
742:     /// ```
743:     pub fn remove(&mut self, index: usize) {
744:         assert!(
745:             index < self.next_index,
746:             "index out of bounds; {} >= {}",
747:             index,
748:             self.next_index,
749:         );
750: 
751:         let i = self
752:             .handles
753:             .iter()
754:             .enumerate()
755:             .find(|(_, (_, i, _))| *i == index)
756:             .expect("no operation with this index")
757:             .0;
758: 
759:         self.handles.swap_remove(i);
760:     }
761: 
762:     /// Attempts to select one of the operations without blocking.
763:     ///
764:     /// If an operation is ready, it is selected and returned. If multiple operations are ready at
765:     /// the same time, a random one among them is selected. If none of the operations are ready, an
766:     /// error is returned.
767:     ///
768:     /// An operation is considered to be ready if it doesn't have to block. Note that it is ready
769:     /// even when it will simply return an error because the channel is disconnected.
770:     ///
771:     /// The selected operation must be completed with [`SelectedOperation::send`]
772:     /// or [`SelectedOperation::recv`].
773:     ///
774:     /// # Examples
775:     ///
776:     /// ```
777:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{unbounded, Select};
778:     ///
779:     /// let (s1, r1) = unbounded();
780:     /// let (s2, r2) = unbounded();
781:     ///
782:     /// s1.send(10).unwrap();
783:     /// s2.send(20).unwrap();
784:     ///
785:     /// let mut sel = Select::new();
786:     /// let oper1 = sel.recv(&r1);
787:     /// let oper2 = sel.recv(&r2);
788:     ///
789:     /// // Both operations are initially ready, so a random one will be executed.
790:     /// let oper = sel.try_select();
791:     /// match oper {
792:     ///     Err(_) => panic!("both operations should be ready"),
793:     ///     Ok(oper) => match oper.index() {
794:     ///         i if i == oper1 => assert_eq!(oper.recv(&r1), Ok(10)),

796:     ///         _ => unreachable!(),
797:     ///     }
798:     /// }
799:     /// ```
800:     pub fn try_select(&mut self) -> Result<SelectedOperation<'a>, TrySelectError> {
801:         try_select(&mut self.handles, self.biased)
802:     }

1196: ///
1197: /// [`send`]: SelectedOperation::send
1198: /// [`recv`]: SelectedOperation::recv
1199: #[must_use]
1200: pub struct SelectedOperation<'a> {

1202:     token: Token,
1203:

1215:     /// Returns the index of the selected operation.
1216:     ///
1217:     /// # Examples
1218:     ///
1219:     /// ```
1220:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{bounded, Select};
1221:     ///
1222:     /// let (s1, r1) = bounded::<()>(0);
1223:     /// let (s2, r2) = bounded::<()>(0);
1224:     /// let (s3, r3) = bounded::<()>(1);
1225:     ///
1226:     /// let mut sel = Select::new();
1227:     /// let oper1 = sel.send(&s1);
1228:     /// let oper2 = sel.recv(&r2);
1229:     /// let oper3 = sel.send(&s3);
1230:     ///
1231:     /// // Only the last operation is ready.
1232:     /// let oper = sel.select();
1233:     /// assert_eq!(oper.index(), 2);
1234:     /// assert_eq!(oper.index(), oper3);
1235:     ///
1236:     /// // Complete the operation.
1237:     /// oper.send(&s3, ()).unwrap();
1238:     /// ```
1239:     pub fn index(&self) -> usize {
1240:         self.index
1241:     }
1242: 
1243:     /// Completes the send operation.
1244:     ///
1245:     /// The passed [`Sender`] reference must be the same one that was used in [`Select::send`]
1246:     /// when the operation was added.
1247:     ///
1248:     /// # Panics
1249:     ///
1250:     /// Panics if an incorrect [`Sender`] reference is passed.
1251:     ///
1252:     /// # Examples
1253:     ///
1254:     /// ```
1255:     /// use crossbeam_channel::{bounded, Select, SendError};
1256:     ///
1257:     /// let (s, r) = bounded::<i32>(0);
1258:     /// drop(r);
1259:     ///

1261:     /// let oper1 = sel.send(&s);

1265:     /// assert_eq!(oper.send(&s, 10), Err(SendError(10)));
1266:     /// ```
1267:     pub fn send<T>(mut self, s: &Sender<T>, msg: T) -> Result<(), SendError<T>> {
1268:         assert!(
1269:             s as *const Sender<T> as *const u8 == self.ptr,
1270:             "passed a sender that wasn't selected",
1271:         );

1301:     pub fn recv<T>(mut self, r: &Receiver<T>) -> Result<T, RecvError> {

1303:             r as *const Receiver<T> as *const u8 == self.ptr,

part.text: Now let me read the `select.rs` and `select_macro.rs` files, plus the `counter` module to understand the inner pointer types.

## Goal

Fix crossbeam-channel issue #1096: A `SelectedOperation` registered with one `Sender` or `Receiver` instance currently panics when completed using a *clone* of that handle, even though both handles belong to the same channel. Completion with a cloned handle from the same channel must work for unbounded, bounded, and zero-capacity channels, while a handle from a *different* channel must still be rejected. The fix must cover both the `Select` API and the `select!` macro machinery.

## Instructions

- Work offline, do not inspect git history or any remote source
- Do **not** modify tests or documentation
- Implement a production-quality fix
- Run relevant tests before finishing

## Discoveries

**Root cause:** In `select.rs`, `SelectedOperation::send()` and `SelectedOperation::recv()` validate the handle by comparing the address of the passed `Sender`/`Receiver` against `self.ptr` (the address stored at registration time). Since cloned `Sender`/`Receiver` handles are separate objects at different memory addresses, this pointer equality check fails.

**Fix approach:** Replace the raw handle address (`*const u8`) stored in `SelectedOperation.ptr` and in the `select!` macro handle tuples with a pointer to the **inner channel object** (the `counter::Sender<T>` or `counter::Receiver<T>` behind the flavor enum). Cloned `Sender`/`Receiver` handles share the same inner channel object, so comparing these inner pointers will correctly identify same-channel handles.

**Key type structure learned from reading `channel.rs`:**
- `Sender<T>` has `flavor: SenderFlavor<T>`
- `SenderFlavor<T>` has three variants: `Array(counter::Sender<flavors::array::Channel<T>>)`, `List(counter::Sender<flavors::list::Channel<T>>)`, `Zero(counter::Sender<flavors::zero::Channel<T>>)`
- `Receiver<T>` has `flavor: ReceiverFlavor<T>`
- `ReceiverFlavor<T>` has six variants: `Array(counter::Receiver<flavors::array::Channel<T>>)`, `List(counter::Receiver<flavors::list::Channel<T>>)`, `Zero(counter::Receiver<flavors::zero::Channel<T>>)`, `At(Arc<flavors::at::Channel>)`, `Tick(Arc<flavors::tick::Channel>)`, `Never(flavors::never::Channel<T>)`
- `same_channel()` comparisons show that for `counter::Sender`/`counter::Receiver` types, equality comparison (`a == b`) works across clones (they share the same reference-counted inner object)
- `same_channel()` for `At`/`Tick` uses `Arc::ptr_eq(a, b)`
- `Never` always returns `true` since there's only one instance

**Files already read and analyzed:**

<content>1: //! Reference counter for channels.
2: 
3: use alloc::boxed::Box;
4: use core::{
5:     ops,
6:     ptr::NonNull,
7:     sync::atomic::{AtomicBool, AtomicUsize, Ordering},
8: };
9: use std::process;
10: 
11: /// Reference counter internals.
12: struct Counter<C> {
13:     /// The number of senders associated with the channel.
14:     senders: AtomicUsize,
15: 
16:     /// The number of receivers associated with the channel.
17:     receivers: AtomicUsize,
18: 
19:     /// Set to `true` if the last sender or the last receiver reference deallocates the channel.
20:     destroy: AtomicBool,
21: 
22:     /// The internal channel.
23:     chan: C,
24: }
25: 
26: /// Wraps a channel into the reference counter.
27: pub(crate) fn new<C>(chan: C) -> (Sender<C>, Receiver<C>) {
28:     let counter = NonNull::from(Box::leak(Box::new(Counter {
29:         senders: AtomicUsize::new(1),
30:         receivers: AtomicUsize::new(1),
31:         destroy: AtomicBool::new(false),
32:         chan,
33:     })));
34:     let s = Sender { counter };
35:     let r = Receiver { counter };
36:     (s, r)
37: }
38: 
39: /// The sending side.
40: pub(crate) struct Sender<C> {
41:     counter: NonNull<Counter<C>>,
42: }
43: 
44: impl<C> Sender<C> {
45:     /// Returns the internal `Counter`.
46:     fn counter(&self) -> &Counter<C> {
47:         unsafe { self.counter.as_ref() }
48:     }
49: 
50:     /// Acquires another sender reference.
51:     pub(crate) fn acquire(&self) -> Self {
52:         let count = self.counter().senders.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
53: 
54:         // Cloning senders and calling `mem::forget` on the clones could potentially overflow the
55:         // counter. It's very difficult to recover sensibly from such degenerate scenarios so we
56:         // just abort when the count becomes very large.
57:         if count > isize::MAX as usize {
58:             process::abort();
59:         }
60: 
61:         Self {
62:             counter: self.counter,
63:         }
64:     }
65: 
66:     /// Releases the sender reference.
67:     ///
68:     /// Function `disconnect` will be called if this is the last sender reference.
69:     pub(crate) unsafe fn release<F: FnOnce(&C) -> bool>(&self, disconnect: F) {
70:         if self.counter().senders.fetch_sub(1, Ordering::AcqRel) == 1 {
71:             disconnect(&self.counter().chan);
72: 
73:             if self.counter().destroy.swap(true, Ordering::AcqRel) {
74:                 drop(unsafe { Box::from_raw(self.counter.as_ptr()) });
75:             }
76:         }
77:     }
78: }
79: 
80: impl<C> ops::Deref for Sender<C> {
81:     type Target = C;
82: 
83:     fn deref(&self) -> &C {
84:         &self.counter().chan
85:     }
86: }
87: 
88: impl<C> PartialEq for Sender<C> {
89:     fn eq(&self, other: &Self) -> bool {
90:         self.counter == other.counter
91:     }
92: }
93: 
94: /// The receiving side.
95: pub(crate) struct Receiver<C> {
96:     counter: NonNull<Counter<C>>,
97: }
98: 
99: impl<C> Receiver<C> {
100:     /// Returns the internal `Counter`.
101:     fn counter(&self) -> &Counter<C> {
102:         unsafe { self.counter.as_ref() }
103:     }
104: 
105:     /// Acquires another receiver reference.
106:     pub(crate) fn acquire(&self) -> Self {
107:         let count = self.counter().receivers.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
108: 
109:         // Cloning receivers and calling `mem::forget` on the clones could potentially overflow the
110:         // counter. It's very difficult to recover sensibly from such degenerate scenarios so we
111:         // just abort when the count becomes very large.
112:         if count > isize::MAX as usize {
113:             process::abort();
114:         }
115: 
116:         Self {
117:             counter: self.counter,
118:         }
119:     }
120: 
121:     /// Releases the receiver reference.
122:     ///
123:     /// Function `disconnect` will be called if this is the last receiver reference.
124:     pub(crate) unsafe fn release<F: FnOnce(&C) -> bool>(&self, disconnect: F) {
125:         if self.counter().receivers.fetch_sub(1, Ordering::AcqRel) == 1 {
126:             disconnect(&self.counter().chan);
127: 
128:             if self.counter().destroy.swap(true, Ordering::AcqRel) {
129:                 drop(unsafe { Box::from_raw(self.counter.as_ptr()) });
130:             }
131:         }
132:     }
133: }
134: 
135: impl<C> ops::Deref for Receiver<C> {
136:     type Target = C;
137: 
138:     fn deref(&self) -> &C {
139:         &self.counter().chan
140:     }
141: }
142: 
143: impl<C> PartialEq for Receiver<C> {
144:     fn eq(&self, other: &Self) -> bool {
145:         self.counter == other.counter
146:     }
147: }

(End of file - total 147 lines)
